children.name: Menura
children.description: Lyrebirds — ancient Australian passerines renowned as the world's foremost avian mimics. The male's extraordinary tail, shaped like a Greek lyre when fanned, is the most elaborate of any bird. They can imitate any sound — from other birds' songs to chainsaws, camera shutters, and car alarms — with astonishing fidelity. Despite their superb mimicry, lyrebirds are rarely seen, spending most of their time scratching through forest leaf litter for invertebrates.

children.name: Menura novaehollandiae
children.description: The most famous lyrebird, found in the wet forests of southeastern Australia. The male's spectacular tail consists of sixteen highly modified feathers, including two outer lyrates shaped like a lyre and filmy silver plumes. Its song includes flawless imitations of at least 20 other bird species. David Attenborough famously featured this species in his Life of Birds series.

children.name: Menura alberti
children.description: Smaller and rarer than the Superb Lyrebird, restricted to a small area of subtropical rainforest in southern Queensland and northern New South Wales. Has a shorter, less elaborate tail. Named after Prince Albert, consort of Queen Victoria.
